---
title: "07 · Trigonometry — The Ratios"
subtitle: "Three ratios in a right triangle — sin, cos, tan"
lesson-id: "t3.trig-ratios"
tier: 3
prerequisites: ["t2.pythagorean-theorem", "t1.ratio-proportion", "t2.similarity-scale"]
unlocks: ["t3.unit-circle"]
applied-domains: ["game-dev", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 30
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [T2 L10 — Pythagoras](../../tier-2-prealgebra/10-pythagorean-theorem/index.qmd)
   and [T2 L13 — Similarity](../../tier-2-prealgebra/13-similarity-scale/index.qmd)?
2. Comfortable with [T1 L09 — Ratio](../../tier-1-foundations/09-ratio-proportion/index.qmd)?
3. Can you identify the **hypotenuse** (longest side, opposite right
   angle) of a right triangle?
:::

## Touch

Adjust angle $\theta$ in a right triangle. Watch sine, cosine, tangent
update.

```{ojs}
viewof thetaDeg = Inputs.range([0, 90], {value: 30, step: 5, label: "Angle θ (degrees):"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const W = 480, H = 360, scale = 200, ox = 60, oy = 280;
  const t = thetaDeg * Math.PI / 180;
  const adj = Math.cos(t) * scale;
  const opp = Math.sin(t) * scale;
  // hypotenuse goes from (ox, oy) toward (ox + adj, oy - opp)
  const A = {x: ox, y: oy};                  // vertex of angle
  const B = {x: ox + adj, y: oy};            // right angle (along x)
  const C = {x: ox + adj, y: oy - opp};      // top of triangle

  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("viewBox", `0 0 ${W} ${H}`)
    .attr("style", "width:100%; max-width:520px; display:block; margin: 0 auto; background:#fafafa;");
  // Triangle
  svg.append("polygon").attr("points", `${A.x},${A.y} ${B.x},${B.y} ${C.x},${C.y}`)
    .attr("fill", "#bbdefb").attr("opacity", 0.4).attr("stroke", "#1f2933").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  // Right-angle marker
  svg.append("rect").attr("x", B.x - 10).attr("y", B.y - 10).attr("width", 10).attr("height", 10).attr("fill", "none").attr("stroke", "#1f2933");
  // Angle arc
  const arcR = 30;
  const arcEndX = ox + arcR * Math.cos(-t), arcEndY = oy + arcR * Math.sin(-t);
  svg.append("path").attr("d", `M ${ox + arcR},${oy} A ${arcR},${arcR} 0 0 0 ${arcEndX},${arcEndY}`)
    .attr("fill", "none").attr("stroke", "#c2185b").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", ox + 50).attr("y", oy - 8).attr("font-size", "14").attr("fill", "#c2185b").attr("font-weight", "bold").text(`θ = ${thetaDeg}°`);

  // Side labels
  svg.append("text").attr("x", (A.x + B.x)/2).attr("y", oy + 18).attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "12").attr("fill", "#2e7d32").text(`adjacent ≈ ${adj.toFixed(1)}`);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", B.x + 8).attr("y", (B.y + C.y)/2).attr("font-size", "12").attr("fill", "#1565c0").text(`opposite ≈ ${opp.toFixed(1)}`);
  // Hypotenuse label
  const midX = (A.x + C.x)/2, midY = (A.y + C.y)/2;
  svg.append("text").attr("x", midX - 70).attr("y", midY).attr("font-size", "12").attr("fill", "#c62828").text(`hypotenuse = ${scale}`);

  // Ratios
  svg.append("text").attr("x", 290).attr("y", 50).attr("font-size", "14").attr("font-weight", "bold").text("Ratios:");
  svg.append("text").attr("x", 290).attr("y", 75).attr("font-size", "13").attr("font-family", "monospace").attr("fill", "#1565c0").text(`sin θ = opp / hyp = ${(opp/scale).toFixed(3)}`);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", 290).attr("y", 95).attr("font-size", "13").attr("font-family", "monospace").attr("fill", "#2e7d32").text(`cos θ = adj / hyp = ${(adj/scale).toFixed(3)}`);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", 290).attr("y", 115).attr("font-size", "13").attr("font-family", "monospace").attr("fill", "#c2185b").text(`tan θ = opp / adj = ${(opp/adj).toFixed(3)}`);
  return svg.node();
}
```

In a right triangle, three ratios depend only on the angle (by
similarity, [T2 L13](../../tier-2-prealgebra/13-similarity-scale/index.qmd)
— scale the triangle and ratios stay the same):

- **sine** = opposite / hypotenuse
- **cosine** = adjacent / hypotenuse
- **tangent** = opposite / adjacent = sin/cos

## Wonder

1. Set $\theta = 0°$. What are sin, cos, tan? Set $\theta = 90°$. What
   happens to tan?
2. As $\theta$ grows from 0° to 90°, sine grows from 0 to 1 and cosine
   shrinks from 1 to 0. Why those bounds?
3. Two different right triangles with the same $\theta$ have different
   side *lengths* but identical sin/cos/tan. Why?

## Discover

### Problem 1

Compute sin, cos, tan of **30°** using a 30-60-90 triangle (hypotenuse
2, sides 1 and $\sqrt{3}$).

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Opposite to 30°: short leg (length 1). Adjacent: long leg ($\sqrt{3}$).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sin 30° = 1/2$, $\cos 30° = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.866$, $\tan 30° =
1/\sqrt{3} \approx 0.577$.

These are **exact** values — memorise them. The 30-60-90 triangle is
*the* canonical example, half of an equilateral triangle.
</details>

### Problem 2

Compute sin, cos, tan of **45°** using a 45-45-90 triangle (legs 1,
hypotenuse $\sqrt{2}$).

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sin 45° = \cos 45° = 1/\sqrt{2} = \sqrt{2}/2 \approx 0.707$. $\tan
45° = 1$.

Sin and cos are equal at 45° — by symmetry of the isoceles right
triangle.
</details>

### Problem 3

A ladder leans against a wall at angle 60° to the ground. The base is
3 m from the wall. How long is the ladder?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Adjacent (along ground) = 3 m. Hypotenuse = ladder. Use cos.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\cos 60° = 3/L \Rightarrow L = 3 / 0.5 = 6$ m.

Trig solves "given an angle and one side, find another side" — a
universal triangle problem.
</details>

### Problem 4

A tree casts a 12-m shadow. The angle of elevation of the sun is 35°.
How tall is the tree?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Opposite (height) = $h$, adjacent (shadow) = 12. Use tan.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tan 35° = h/12 \Rightarrow h = 12 \tan 35° \approx 12 \cdot 0.700
\approx 8.4$ m.

The "shadow" problem (T2 L13 P3) generalised: instead of comparing
two known shadows, use a known angle.
</details>

### Problem 5

Use the **Pythagorean identity** $\sin^2 \theta + \cos^2 \theta = 1$
(a renaming of Pythagoras applied to opp/hyp and adj/hyp). Verify for
$\theta = 30°$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$(1/2)^2 + (\sqrt{3}/2)^2 = ?$
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$1/4 + 3/4 = 1$ ✓.

Why? In a right triangle, $\text{opp}^2 + \text{adj}^2 = \text{hyp}^2$
(Pythagoras). Divide both sides by $\text{hyp}^2$: $\sin^2 \theta +
\cos^2 \theta = 1$.

This is the **most-used trig identity** of all time. We meet a flock
of related identities in [Lesson 09](../09-trig-identities/index.qmd).
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
The word *sine* is one of the strangest accidents in mathematics.
The Indian astronomer **Āryabhaṭa** (c. 500 CE) tabulated the
half-chord of a circular arc and called it *jyā* (Sanskrit for
"bowstring"). Persian translators wrote it as *jiba*; medieval
Latin scribes mis-read the Arabic consonants as *jaib*, "fold" or
"bay" — and translated *that* into Latin as *sinus*, which became
English *sine*. So whenever you write $\sin\theta$, you are
quoting a 1,500-year-old Sanskrit word for "bowstring," twice
mistranslated.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
Sin, cos, and tan are *not* numbers attached to triangles — they
are numbers attached to *angles*. Two right triangles with the
same angle, however different in size, share the same trig
ratios. That's why a single sine table fits any triangle in the
universe.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

For a right triangle with angle $\theta$ (one of the non-right
angles):

$$\sin \theta = \dfrac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}}, \quad
  \cos \theta = \dfrac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}}, \quad
  \tan \theta = \dfrac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}}.$$

Mnemonic: **SOH-CAH-TOA** (Sine = Opp/Hyp, Cosine = Adj/Hyp, Tangent
= Opp/Adj).

**Useful exact values:**

| $\theta$ | sin | cos | tan |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 0° | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 30° | 1/2 | $\sqrt{3}/2$ | $1/\sqrt{3}$ |
| 45° | $\sqrt{2}/2$ | $\sqrt{2}/2$ | 1 |
| 60° | $\sqrt{3}/2$ | 1/2 | $\sqrt{3}$ |
| 90° | 1 | 0 | undefined |

**The Pythagorean identity:** $\sin^2 \theta + \cos^2 \theta = 1$.

**Reciprocal ratios:** $\csc \theta = 1/\sin \theta$, $\sec \theta =
1/\cos \theta$, $\cot \theta = 1/\tan \theta$.

:::

> *SOH-CAH-TOA — opp over hyp, adj over hyp, opp over adj;*
> *Three ratios that depend only on the angle, not the size;*
> *Sin² + cos² = 1, the Pythagorean identity in trig form.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> A right triangle has hypotenuse 10 cm and one angle 25°. Find the
> opposite and adjacent sides.

A competent solver:

1. **Opposite:** $\sin 25° = \text{opp}/10 \Rightarrow \text{opp} = 10
   \sin 25° \approx 10 \cdot 0.4226 \approx 4.23$ cm.
2. **Adjacent:** $\cos 25° = \text{adj}/10 \Rightarrow \text{adj} \approx
   10 \cdot 0.9063 \approx 9.06$ cm.
3. **Check Pythagoras:** $4.23^2 + 9.06^2 \approx 17.9 + 82.1 \approx
   100 = 10^2$ ✓.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** $\sin 30°$? $\cos 60°$? $\tan 45°$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$1/2$, $1/2$, $1$.
</details>

**W2.** A right triangle has angle 40° and adjacent side 8 cm. Find
the opposite.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tan 40° = \text{opp}/8 \Rightarrow \text{opp} \approx 8 \cdot 0.839
\approx 6.71$ cm.
</details>

**W3.** Verify $\sin^2 60° + \cos^2 60° = 1$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(\sqrt{3}/2)^2 + (1/2)^2 = 3/4 + 1/4 = 1$ ✓.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** A 5-m ladder leans against a wall, top at 4 m up. What angle
does it make with the ground?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\sin \theta = 4/5 \Rightarrow \theta = \arcsin(0.8)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\theta = \arcsin(0.8) \approx 53.13°$.

(arcsin / sin⁻¹ is the inverse: "what angle has sine 0.8?")
</details>

**P2.** A plane climbs at angle 12° to the horizontal. After flying 5
km along its path, how much altitude has it gained?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sin 12° = h/5 \Rightarrow h = 5 \sin 12° \approx 5 \cdot 0.208
\approx 1.04$ km. The plane is now ~1.04 km higher.
</details>

**P3.** Find $\sin \theta$ if $\cos \theta = 0.6$ and $\theta$ is in
$(0, 90°)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Use $\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sin^2 \theta = 1 - 0.36 = 0.64 \Rightarrow \sin \theta = 0.8$
(positive in this quadrant).

This is a 3-4-5 triangle in disguise! cos = 3/5 = 0.6, sin = 4/5 = 0.8.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** **3D direction in a game.** A character looks at angle 60°
above horizontal toward the north. What are the components of their
view-direction *unit vector* (sin, cos with appropriate axis
mappings)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
With north as +y, up as +z: forward component = cos 60° = 0.5;
upward component = sin 60° = 0.866. East/west: 0.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
View vector: $(0, \cos 60°, \sin 60°) = (0, 0.5, 0.866)$.

Game engines compute look-direction vectors from camera angles by
exactly these trig formulas, millions of times per second. Direction
encoding via sin/cos is *the* universal solution for "rotate this
object in 3D".
</details>

**S2.** **The fundamental theorem of trigonometry** (informal):
*every wave can be built from sines and cosines.* Sound, light, AC
electricity, brain activity, ocean tides — all decomposed into sums of
sin/cos. This decomposition is called **Fourier analysis** (Tier 6).
Why might sin/cos be the "right" basis?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Sin/cos are the natural building blocks of periodic motion.** A point
moving uniformly around a circle traces sin (vertical position) and
cos (horizontal position) over time — the simplest oscillation in
existence.

When you decompose a complex sound into "pure tones," each tone is a
sine wave. The cochlea in your ear physically performs Fourier
decomposition (different hair cells respond to different
frequencies). MP3, JPEG, MRI imaging, radio, and Wi-Fi all rely on
Fourier methods. The sin/cos you just learned are the fundamental
language of every wave on Earth.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Sin/cos must be in degrees."** They can be in *radians* too
   (Lesson 08). Calculators have a mode switch.
2. **"Tan can be any value."** True — tan ranges over all real numbers
   (unlike sin and cos which are bounded between $-1$ and $1$). At
   $90°, 270°, \ldots$, tan is undefined (division by zero).
3. **"Trig only works in right triangles."** Right-triangle definitions
   only work for $0 < \theta < 90°$. The unit circle (Lesson 08)
   extends them to *all angles*.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **Sine, cosine, and tangent are three [ratios](/glossary.qmd#ratio)
   of sides in a [right-angled](/glossary.qmd#right-angle)
   [triangle](/glossary.qmd#triangle) — they depend on the
   [angle](/glossary.qmd#angle), not the size.**
2. **SOH-CAH-TOA: Sine = Opp/[Hyp](/glossary.qmd#hypotenuse), Cosine =
   Adj/Hyp, Tangent = Opp/Adj — that one mnemonic carries every
   right-triangle problem.**
3. **The Pythagorean identity $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$ is
   just Pythagoras divided through by the hypotenuse squared — the
   geometry shows up unchanged in the algebra.**

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you derive the Pythagorean identity from Pythagoras' theorem?
2. Without scrolling back, what is $\sin 30° \cdot \cos 60°$?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answer: $(1/2)(1/2) = 1/4$.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[08 · The Unit Circle](../08-unit-circle/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S2** — the Fourier connection is one of maths'
  most powerful unifications.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P1, P2, P5*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[T2 L10 — Pythagoras](../../tier-2-prealgebra/10-pythagorean-theorem/index.qmd)**
- **[T1 L09 — Ratio](../../tier-1-foundations/09-ratio-proportion/index.qmd)** —
  trig values *are* ratios.
- **[T2 L13 — Similarity](../../tier-2-prealgebra/13-similarity-scale/index.qmd)** —
  scale-invariance of the ratios.

### What this unlocks

- **[08 · Unit Circle](../08-unit-circle/index.qmd)** — extends trig
  beyond right triangles to all angles.
- **Tier 4 — Calculus & Tier 6 — Fourier Analysis** — sin/cos as
  the *building blocks of periodic phenomena*.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Camera rotations, character orientation, view
vectors (S1), arc projectiles, oscillating springs in physics — all
sin/cos. Quaternions (Tier 7) build on this for full 3D rotations.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Engineering & physics.** AC electricity is a sine wave (50 or 60
Hz). Sound is a superposition of sines. Light is sinusoidal in
amplitude. Bridges and buildings are designed against sinusoidal
wind/seismic loads.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Mathematical research.** Trigonometric functions are the simplest
nontrivial *periodic* functions. Their connection to complex
exponentials ($e^{i\theta} = \cos \theta + i \sin \theta$, Tier 6)
unifies analysis with algebra in a deep way.
:::
